whyWrongByChoice.B: apoA-I activates LCAT on HDL, not lipoprotein lipase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: apoB-48 is a structural protein of chylomicrons and does not activate LPL.
whyWrongByChoice.D: apoB-100 is a structural protein of VLDL/LDL and binds the LDL receptor rather than activating LPL.
whyWrongByChoice.E: apoE mediates hepatic remnant uptake, not activation of lipoprotein lipase.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction

questions.stem: In the fed state, long-chain fatty acids cannot enter the mitochondria for beta-oxidation, ensuring that fatty acid synthesis and degradation do not occur simultaneously. Which molecule, a product of fatty acid synthesis, allosterically inhibits carnitine acyltransferase-1 (CPT1) to prevent this entry?

explanation.summary: Malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT1, blocking long-chain fatty acid entry into mitochondria during active synthesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: When fatty acid synthesis is active, acetyl-CoA carboxylase generates elevated malonyl-CoA, which allosterically inhibits carnitine acyltransferase-1. This prevents long-chain fatty acids from being shuttled into the mitochondrial matrix for beta-oxidation, keeping synthesis and degradation asynchronous.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Palmitoyl-CoA provides negative feedback on acetyl-CoA carboxylase, not the CPT1 inhibition described here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acetyl-CoA is the building block for synthesis, not the inhibitor of CPT1.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Citrate carries acetyl units to the cytosol and activates acetyl-CoA carboxylase; it does not inhibit CPT1.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Methylmalonyl-CoA is an intermediate in odd-chain fatty acid catabolism and does not regulate CPT1.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction

questions.stem: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase catalyzes the committed, rate-limiting step of fatty acid synthesis by carboxylating acetyl-CoA to malonyl-CoA. This reaction requires a specific vitamin-derived cofactor. Which vitamin serves as the cofactor for this carboxylation?

explanation.summary: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase uses biotin (vitamin B7) to add CO2 to acetyl-CoA.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase produces malonyl-CoA from cytosolic acetyl-CoA by adding a CO2 in a biotin (vitamin B7)-dependent manner, consistent with biotin's general role as a cofactor for carboxylase enzymes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cobalamin is the cofactor for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase in odd-chain fatty acid catabolism, not for acetyl-CoA carboxylase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thiamine is a cofactor for dehydrogenase/transketolase reactions, not for this carboxylation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin E is a fat-soluble antioxidant with no role as a carboxylase cofactor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Niacin gives rise to NAD/NADP but is not the carboxylase cofactor here.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction

questions.stem: In hepatocytes, surplus glucose-derived acetyl-CoA generated in the mitochondria must reach the cytosol to serve as the building block for fatty acid synthesis. Because there is no direct acetyl-CoA transporter, acetyl-CoA is exported in the form of which intermediate that is then cleaved in the cytosol?

explanation.summary: Acetyl-CoA is exported to the cytosol as citrate, which is cleaved by citrate lyase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mitochondrial acetyl-CoA is condensed with oxaloacetate to citrate, a TCA intermediate that is exported to the cytosol. There citrate lyase cleaves it back to oxaloacetate (which continues the shuttle) and acetyl-CoA, which is used for fatty acid synthesis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Malate is part of the return arm of the citrate shuttle in the cytosol, not the carrier that exports the acetyl unit.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pyruvate is the return product that re-enters mitochondria; it does not carry the acetyl unit out.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oxaloacetate cannot cross the inner mitochondrial membrane directly and is regenerated in the cytosol after citrate is cleaved.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Malonyl-CoA is formed in the cytosol from acetyl-CoA after the shuttle; it is not the export form.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction

questions.stem: During beta-oxidation of an odd-numbered fatty acid, the final three-carbon product propionyl-CoA is converted to succinyl-CoA so it can enter the TCA cycle. This conversion proceeds through a methylmalonyl-CoA intermediate and requires a specific vitamin. Deficiency of which vitamin would impair this step?

explanation.summary: Methylmalonyl-CoA mutase requires cobalamin (B12) to convert propionyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA.
explanation.whyCorrect: Odd-chain fatty acids yield propionyl-CoA, which is converted to succinyl-CoA through a methylmalonyl-CoA intermediate. Methylmalonyl-CoA mutase requires cobalamin, so a B12 deficiency impairs this conversion.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Biotin is the cofactor for carboxylases such as acetyl-CoA carboxylase, not for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Folate participates in one-carbon transfers for nucleotide synthesis, not this mutase reaction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin K is required for gamma-carboxylation of clotting factors, unrelated to propionyl-CoA metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pyridoxine supports transamination and other reactions but is not the cofactor for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction

questions.stem: A 14-month-old boy presents with poor feeding, muscle weakness, an enlarged and weakened heart (cardiomyopathy), and hypoglycemia. Genetic testing identifies a mutation in SLC22A5 encoding the OCTN2 transporter, impairing cellular carnitine uptake. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?

explanation.summary: Primary carnitine deficiency from OCTN2 (SLC22A5) mutation is treated with L-carnitine supplementation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mutations in SLC22A5 impair the OCTN2 transporter that imports carnitine, causing primary carnitine deficiency with encephalopathy, cardiomyopathy, weakness, and hypoglycemia in infancy or early childhood. Depending on the variant, supplementation with L-carnitine can be beneficial by restoring the substrate needed for the carnitine shuttle.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A high-fat diet increases reliance on the impaired fatty acid oxidation pathway and would not correct the carnitine deficit.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Biotin addresses carboxylase reactions and would not replace deficient carnitine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cobalamin is needed for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, not for carnitine transport.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Restricting carbohydrate would worsen energy failure, since carbohydrate is the safer fuel when fat oxidation is impaired.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with chronic pancreatitis has ongoing fat malabsorption producing bulky, greasy, foul-smelling stools (steatorrhea). Given that lipoproteins carry certain vitamins in their hydrophobic core, deficiency of which group of vitamins is the major concern in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K

explanation.summary: Fat malabsorption and steatorrhea threaten the fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dietary fat malabsorption produces steatorrhea, and because the fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K are absorbed and carried within lipoproteins along with dietary lipid, their depletion is the major concern when fat absorption fails.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The B vitamins are water-soluble and are not lost preferentially with dietary fat.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Vitamin C is water-soluble and absorbed independently of dietary fat.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Folate and cobalamin are water-soluble vitamins not carried in the hydrophobic lipoprotein core.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Niacin and thiamine are water-soluble B vitamins unaffected by fat malabsorption in this way.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction

questions.stem: A researcher studies the endogenous pathway that converts acetyl-CoA into cholesterol in hepatocytes. She wishes to target the single step that is rate-limiting and is exploited by a major class of lipid-lowering drugs. Which enzyme should she target?

explanation.summary: HMG-CoA reductase catalyzes the rate-limiting step of cholesterol synthesis and is the target of statins.
explanation.whyCorrect: HMG-CoA reductase reduces HMG-CoA to mevalonate using NADPH and is the rate-limiting, most clinically salient step of cytoplasmic cholesterol synthesis. Statins competitively inhibit this enzyme to lower hepatic cholesterol production.

whyWrongByChoice.A: HMG-CoA lyase cleaves HMG-CoA to acetoacetate in ketone body synthesis, not cholesterol synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HMG-CoA synthase forms HMG-CoA but does not catalyze the rate-limiting reduction and is central to ketogenesis in the mitochondrial matrix.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thiolase condenses two acetyl-CoA into acetoacetyl-CoA and is not the rate-limiting or drug-targeted step.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Squalene is a downstream precursor of cholesterol; its formation is not the rate-limiting, statin-targeted step.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism: Cholesterol Synthesis and Ketone Bodies

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man is started on a statin for hypercholesterolemia. Several weeks later his plasma LDL is markedly reduced. Which of the following mechanisms best explains the fall in plasma LDL?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct binding and clearance of LDL particles by the drug

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased intestinal absorption of dietary cholesterol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enhanced biliary excretion of free cholesterol

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhibition of lipoprotein lipase in capillaries

explanation.summary: By lowering hepatic cholesterol synthesis, statins drive the liver to upregulate LDL receptors, which clears LDL from plasma.
explanation.whyCorrect: When statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, hepatic cholesterol production drops while the liver still needs cholesterol for processes like bile salt synthesis. Hepatocytes compensate by increasing LDL receptor expression on their surface to take up cholesterol from the circulation, and this increased receptor expression is what lowers plasma LDL.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Statins do not physically bind or clear LDL particles; they act on an intracellular enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Statins act on hepatic synthesis, not on intestinal cholesterol absorption.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The brick attributes LDL lowering to hepatic LDL receptor upregulation, not to increased biliary cholesterol excretion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lipoprotein lipase hydrolyzes triglycerides in lipoproteins and is not the mechanism of statin-induced LDL lowering.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism: Cholesterol Synthesis and Ketone Bodies

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman taking a statin develops diffuse muscle aches without weakness. Research on statin-associated myalgia points to a deficiency of a specific molecule in muscle tissue. Which molecule is most likely reduced?

explanation.summary: Statin-related muscle pain is linked to suboptimal coenzyme Q (ubiquinone) in muscle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Research shows patients on statins with muscle aches have suboptimal levels of coenzyme Q (ubiquinone), a component of the electron transport chain that shares the mevalonate pathway with cholesterol. Many patients therefore take CoQ supplements, though the evidence for benefit is lacking.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Creatine phosphate is an energy buffer and is not the molecule the brick ties to statin myalgia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thiamine deficiency is discussed with alcoholism and CNS energy failure, not statin muscle pain.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carnitine shuttles fatty acids into mitochondria but is not implicated in statin myalgia here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: NADPH is a reductant used in cholesterol synthesis, not the muscle molecule linked to statin aches.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism: Cholesterol Synthesis and Ketone Bodies

questions.stem: A physiology student is asked which circulating hormone is directly synthesized from cholesterol as its precursor. Which of the following is the best answer?

explanation.summary: Steroid hormones such as testosterone are synthesized from cholesterol.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cholesterol is the precursor of all steroid hormones, including the sex hormones estrogen and testosterone, mineralocorticoids like aldosterone, and glucocorticoids like cortisol. Testosterone is a steroid sex hormone and thus requires cholesterol.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glucagon is a peptide hormone secreted by the pancreas, not a cholesterol derivative.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Insulin is a peptide hormone from pancreatic beta cells and is not built from cholesterol.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thyroid hormone is derived from tyrosine and iodine and is not cholesterol-based.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Epinephrine is a catecholamine derived from tyrosine, not from cholesterol.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism: Cholesterol Synthesis and Ketone Bodies

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man is noted on eye exam to have yellowish plaques on his eyelids and a faint ring around the periphery of his cornea. He also has firm nodular thickening over the Achilles tendon. Given his young age, these findings should most prompt evaluation for which of the following?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A benign, expected consequence of normal aging

explanation.summary: Xanthelasma, corneal arcus, and tendon xanthomas in a young patient suggest an underlying lipid disorder.
explanation.whyCorrect: Xanthomas are lipid-filled foam-cell lesions associated with high cholesterol and metabolic disorders, commonly appearing on tendons of the hands or Achilles. Xanthelasma affects the eyelids and corneal arcus rings the cornea; while these can be benign signs of aging, their appearance in someone younger than 50 is indicative of a lipid disorder.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Corneal arcus and xanthelasma can be benign aging changes, but in a patient under 50 they point to a lipid disorder rather than normal aging.
whyWrongByChoice.C: These cholesterol-laden lesions are not caused by ultraviolet light exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The lesions reflect lipid accumulation, not primary autoimmune thyroid disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tendon xanthomas are chronic lipid deposits, not an acute bacterial tendon infection.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism: Cholesterol Synthesis and Ketone Bodies

questions.stem: During prolonged fasting, hepatocytes synthesize ketone bodies from accumulating acetyl-CoA. In which subcellular location does ketone body synthesis occur, contrasting with cholesterol synthesis?

explanation.summary: Ketone bodies are produced in the mitochondrial matrix of the liver, whereas cholesterol synthesis is cytoplasmic.
explanation.whyCorrect: Although both pathways pass through an HMG-CoA intermediate and require three acetyl-CoA, ketone body formation occurs within the mitochondrial matrix while cholesterol synthesis is localized to the cytoplasm. This compartmental separation lets the two pathways use the same intermediate for different purposes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The cytoplasm is where cholesterol synthesis occurs, not ketone body synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The smooth endoplasmic reticulum is not the site described for ketogenesis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Peroxisomes contribute to very-long-chain fatty acid oxidation but are not the site of ketone body synthesis here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The Golgi apparatus processes and packages proteins and is not involved in ketogenesis.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism: Cholesterol Synthesis and Ketone Bodies

questions.stem: During starvation, skeletal muscle takes up circulating ketone bodies and catabolizes them for energy. Catabolism of ketone bodies in these tissues directly regenerates which molecule that then enters the TCA cycle?

explanation.summary: Peripheral tissues convert ketone bodies back into acetyl-CoA to feed the TCA cycle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ketone bodies are transported without carriers to tissues such as skeletal muscle, cardiac muscle, and renal cortex, which express enzymes that convert them back to acetyl-CoA to supply the TCA cycle. Acetyl-CoA cannot directly give rise to pyruvate or glucose.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pyruvate is a gluconeogenic element; acetyl-CoA from ketolysis cannot be converted directly to pyruvate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glucose is not produced from ketone body catabolism; acetyl-CoA cannot yield net glucose.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Alanine is a gluconeogenic amino acid and is not the product of ketone body catabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Free fatty acid oxidation generates the acetyl-CoA that is packaged into ketone bodies; ketolysis regenerates acetyl-CoA, not fatty acids.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism: Cholesterol Synthesis and Ketone Bodies

questions.stem: The liver synthesizes ketone bodies but does not consume them, avoiding a futile cycle of production and catabolism within the same cells. Nominal hepatic expression of which enzyme accounts for the liver's inability to catabolize ketone bodies?

explanation.summary: The liver expresses only nominal beta-ketoacyl-CoA transferase, so it cannot use the ketone bodies it makes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Utilization of ketone bodies requires beta-ketoacyl-CoA transferase, which converts acetoacetate to acetoacetyl-CoA and circumvents the irreversible synthesis steps. Because the liver expresses this enzyme only nominally, it produces ketone bodies but cannot catabolize them, preventing a futile cycle.

whyWrongByChoice.B: beta-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase is expressed in the liver and tissues to interconvert acetoacetate and beta-hydroxybutyrate based on NADH.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thiolase is present in the liver as the last step of beta-oxidation and the first step of ketone synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HMG-CoA reductase catalyzes the rate-limiting step of cholesterol synthesis, not ketone catabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HMG-CoA synthase is actually a liver ketogenic enzyme, not the one absent from the liver.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism: Cholesterol Synthesis and Ketone Bodies

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with rapid weight loss after several weeks on a strict high-fat, low-carbohydrate diet. Blood pressure is 112/74 mmHg and heart rate is 90/min. Exam shows reduced muscle mass and adipose tissue. Urine shows elevated ketones but no glucose or reducing sugars. Which metabolic adaptation primarily contributes to her ketosis?

explanation.summary: In dietary ketosis, glucagon-driven fatty acid oxidation floods the liver with acetyl-CoA, producing ketone bodies.
explanation.whyCorrect: Her low insulin/glucagon ratio mobilizes triglyceride stores from adipocytes to fuel hepatic gluconeogenesis. Fatty acid oxidation produces acetyl-CoA for the TCA to power gluconeogenesis, and the combination of rising acetyl-CoA and the pulling of oxaloacetate into gluconeogenesis leads to acetyl-CoA accumulation and ketone body production.

whyWrongByChoice.A: There is no evidence of pathologic insulin-signaling disruption in this healthy dieter; her hormonal state is a normal low insulin/glucagon ratio.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Glycolysis would not be heightened when dietary glucose is limited.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lipogenesis is an anabolic process, but she is in a catabolic, fat-mobilizing state.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chylomicron formation follows a high-fat meal and delivers carbons for use, but it is not the adaptation driving her ketosis.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism: Cholesterol Synthesis and Ketone Bodies

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man with a history of alcohol abuse reports drinking large amounts of whiskey over several days. Blood pressure is 100/60 mmHg and respiratory rate is 18/min. Labs show elevated lactate, a high anion gap metabolic acidosis, hypoglycemia, and elevated serum ketones. Which parameter is most directly contributing to his ketone body production?

explanation.summary: Alcohol metabolism raises the hepatic NADH/NAD+ ratio, which slows the TCA and gluconeogenesis and drives ketogenesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alcohol metabolism increases the NADH/NAD+ ratio, favoring conversion of pyruvate to lactate (depriving gluconeogenesis of substrate) and oxaloacetate to malate. This depletes and slows the TCA cycle, so acetyl-CoA accumulates and is shunted into ketone body synthesis, producing hypoglycemia and ketoacidosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Decreased, not sustained, insulin signaling favors ketone body formation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The metabolic acidosis is a result of the accumulating ketones and lactate, not the cause of ketone production.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thiamine deficiency is associated with alcoholism and hypoglycemia management but does not directly cause ketone body formation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pyruvate is actually diverted to lactate and reduced, not increased, in this high-NADH state.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism: Cholesterol Synthesis and Ketone Bodies

questions.stem: A 19-year-old with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes presents in diabetic ketoacidosis. Despite a very high plasma glucose, his tissues behave as if starving. Which underlying abnormality best explains why his body remains in a catabolic, ketone-producing state?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Insulin insufficiency preventing glucose uptake via GLUT4

choices.label: B
choices.text: Excess insulin driving glucose into all tissues

choices.label: C
choices.text: A primary deficiency of glucagon secretion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inability to absorb dietary carbohydrate from the gut

explanation.summary: In DKA, insulin insufficiency prevents insulin-dependent glucose uptake, so tissues sense starvation despite high glucose.
explanation.whyCorrect: The fundamental cause of DKA is insulin insufficiency, most common in type 1 diabetics who cannot synthesize insulin. Insulin is required for tissues like skeletal muscle and adipocytes to take up glucose via GLUT4, so in its absence the body perceives a starving state despite abundant plasma glucose, promoting fatty acid liberation, gluconeogenesis, and ketone body production.

whyWrongByChoice.B: DKA reflects a lack of insulin, not an excess driving glucose into tissues.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DKA arises from insulin deficiency; glucagon signaling is relatively unopposed rather than deficient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Loss of glucose-6-phosphatase would impair glucose release, not explain the insulin-deficient starvation state of DKA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The problem is cellular glucose uptake due to missing insulin, not intestinal carbohydrate absorption.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism: Cholesterol Synthesis and Ketone Bodies

questions.stem: A patient in severe diabetic ketoacidosis exhibits labored, rapid, deep breathing. This respiratory pattern is a compensatory response to the acid load. What is the physiologic purpose of this breathing pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To exhale CO2 and shift the bicarbonate buffer to consume excess protons

choices.label: B
choices.text: To retain CO2 and raise carbonic acid concentration

choices.label: C
choices.text: To exhale ketone bodies directly from the alveoli

choices.label: D
choices.text: To increase oxygen delivery and correct the underlying hypoglycemia

explanation.summary: Kussmaul respirations blow off CO2, driving the bicarbonate buffer toward consuming excess protons to relieve acidosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Accumulating ketone bodies act as metabolic acids and cause a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. Labored deep breathing (Kussmaul respirations) rapidly exhales CO2, shifting the bicarbonate buffering equilibrium (HCO3- + H+ to H2CO3 to CO2 + H2O) so that excess protons are consumed, helping relieve the drop in pH.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Retaining CO2 and raising carbonic acid would worsen the acidosis, the opposite of the compensatory goal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fruity breath comes from volatile acetone, but Kussmaul breathing itself functions to blow off CO2, not to exhale ketone acids as a compensation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The breathing pattern compensates for acidosis and does not correct the hypoglycemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Kussmaul respirations are a pulmonary, not renal, compensatory mechanism for the acid load.

questions.topicName: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism: Cholesterol Synthesis and Ketone Bodies

questions.stem: After a wasp sting, a 30-year-old man develops local swelling and redness. Tissue-resident macrophages and infiltrating neutrophils release an enzyme that liberates the substrate for eicosanoid synthesis from the phospholipids of the cell membrane. Which of the following enzymes is directly responsible for releasing this substrate?

explanation.summary: PLA2 cleaves membrane phospholipids to release arachidonic acid, the substrate for eicosanoid synthesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In response to tissue damage, pro-inflammatory cells such as neutrophils and macrophages release phospholipase A2 (PLA2), which cleaves the sn-2 position of membrane phospholipids and liberates arachidonic acid. Arachidonic acid is the precursor from which all eicosanoids are subsequently generated.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Phospholipase C generates lipid-derived second messengers that alter intracellular calcium levels; it does not release arachidonic acid for eicosanoid synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: COX-1 acts downstream on arachidonic acid after it has already been released; it does not free the fatty acid from the membrane.
whyWrongByChoice.D: LOX also acts downstream on already-released arachidonic acid to make leukotrienes, not on the membrane phospholipid itself.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HMG-CoA reductase is the rate-limiting enzyme of cholesterol synthesis and has no role in liberating arachidonic acid.

questions.topicName: BOOM: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction (3 of 3)

questions.stem: A biochemistry student is asked to characterize arachidonic acid, the key substrate of the inflammatory eicosanoid pathway. Which of the following best describes this molecule?

choices.label: D
choices.text: A sterol with a four fused-ring structure

choices.label: E
choices.text: A phospholipid with a phosphate head group

explanation.summary: Arachidonic acid is a 20-carbon omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid and the precursor of eicosanoids.
explanation.whyCorrect: Arachidonic acid is a 20-carbon omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid that is an abundant membrane component. It is designated omega-6 because its first double bond occurs on the sixth carbon counting from the terminal methyl group, and its 20-carbon length is the origin of the term eicosanoid (Greek eicosa = 20).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Arachidonic acid is unsaturated (it has multiple double bonds) and has 20 carbons, not 16, and it is not saturated.
whyWrongByChoice.C: It is an omega-6, not an omega-3, fatty acid; omega-3 species such as DHA have their first double bond on the third carbon from the methyl end.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A four fused-ring structure describes cholesterol/sterols, not a fatty acid.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Arachidonic acid is a free fatty acid released from phospholipids, not a phospholipid bearing a phosphate head group.

questions.topicName: BOOM: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction (3 of 3)

questions.stem: A researcher notes that a locally produced signaling molecule derived from arachidonic acid exerts its effect only near the cell that made it and disappears within minutes. Which property of eicosanoids best explains this restricted, short-range action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are stored in secretory granules and released only in bulk

choices.label: B
choices.text: They bind intracellular DNA response elements to alter transcription

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are G-protein coupled receptor ligands with a half-life of roughly 10 seconds to 5 minutes

choices.label: D
choices.text: They circulate bound to albumin for hours before acting

choices.label: E
choices.text: They are covalently incorporated into membrane phospholipids for long-term signaling

explanation.summary: Eicosanoids are short-lived GPCR ligands, so they act locally near their site of synthesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Eicosanoids are ligands for G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs) with a short half-life of 10 seconds to 5 minutes. Because they are rapidly degraded, they alter cellular function only within close proximity to where they are synthesized, giving them autocrine/paracrine (local) rather than endocrine (systemic) actions.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Eicosanoids are synthesized on demand from membrane arachidonic acid rather than pre-stored in granules for bulk release.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Eicosanoids act at cell-surface GPCRs, not by binding intracellular DNA elements like steroid hormones do.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Their very short half-life prevents prolonged circulation; they do not act systemically for hours.
whyWrongByChoice.E: They are cleaved from membranes and act as diffusible ligands, not re-incorporated for long-term membrane signaling.

questions.topicName: BOOM: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction (3 of 3)

questions.stem: During an acute inflammatory response, a particular cyclooxygenase isoform is transiently expressed and drives the hallmarks of inflammation: pain, heat, redness, and swelling. Which enzyme is being described?

explanation.summary: COX-2 is the inducible isoform expressed during inflammation and tissue injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: COX-2 is referred to as the inducible form because it is transiently expressed during inflammation or tissue injury and is associated with the hallmarks of inflammation: pain, heat (fever), redness, and swelling. This distinction is the rationale behind developing selective COX-2 inhibitors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: COX-1 is the constitutive form, continuously expressed to maintain physiological homeostasis, not transiently induced by inflammation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: LOX produces leukotrienes and is a separate branch; it is not the induced cyclooxygenase driving these classic inflammatory signs.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PLA2 releases arachidonic acid upstream of COX and is not the inducible cyclooxygenase isoform.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thromboxane synthase converts PGH2 to thromboxane A2 and is not the inducible COX isoform associated with inflammation.

questions.topicName: BOOM: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction (3 of 3)

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with a recent mild stroke is started on low-dose daily aspirin for stroke and heart-attack prevention. Her lipid panel, LDL, HDL, and HMG-CoA reductase function are all unaffected by the drug. Which mechanism best explains aspirin's antithrombotic benefit in this setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reversible competitive inhibition of COX-2 in endothelium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Irreversible inhibition of platelet COX-1, blocking thromboxane A2

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase, lowering circulating LDL

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibition of phospholipase A2, reducing arachidonic acid release

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blockade of leukotriene receptors on platelets

explanation.summary: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits platelet COX-1, abolishing thromboxane A2 production for the entire platelet lifespan.
explanation.whyCorrect: Platelets are essentially anucleate cell fragments that lack organelles and cannot synthesize new protein. Once aspirin irreversibly inhibits COX-1 in a platelet, thromboxane A2 (TXA2) production is blocked for the entire lifespan of that platelet. Reduced platelet-derived TXA2 decreases both platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction, which is clinically beneficial in cardiovascular disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aspirin inhibits COX irreversibly, not reversibly, and its antithrombotic effect is due to platelet COX-1, not endothelial COX-2.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aspirin does not affect HMG-CoA reductase or LDL; the vignette explicitly notes the lipid panel is unchanged.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glucocorticoids, not aspirin, inhibit PLA2; aspirin acts on COX downstream of arachidonic acid release.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aspirin acts on the COX enzyme, not on leukotriene receptors, and leukotrienes are not the antithrombotic mediator here.

questions.topicName: BOOM: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction (3 of 3)

questions.stem: Aspirin covalently modifies a serine residue in the active site of both cyclooxygenase isoforms, and this effect can only be overcome by synthesis of new enzyme. Which best describes this type of enzyme inhibition?

explanation.summary: Aspirin acetylates an active-site serine, irreversibly (suicide) inhibiting COX.
explanation.whyCorrect: Aspirin promotes acetylation, a covalent post-translational modification, of a serine residue within the active site of both COX isoforms. This covalent modification irreversibly inhibits the enzyme by sterically blocking arachidonic acid entry into the active site, so the only way to overcome the inhibition is synthesis of new enzyme, making aspirin a suicide inhibitor.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Competitive inhibitors bind reversibly and can be displaced by substrate; aspirin's covalent acetylation is not reversible.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Noncompetitive inhibition is reversible and does not involve covalent modification of the active site.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Uncompetitive inhibition binds only the enzyme-substrate complex reversibly and does not describe covalent acetylation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Allosteric feedback inhibition is a reversible regulatory mechanism, not a covalent, irreversible modification of the active site.

questions.topicName: BOOM: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction (3 of 3)

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with epigastric pain and bloating. He reports frequent ibuprofen use for chronic back pain over the past 8 months. Examination shows epigastric tenderness without rebound, and labs show mild normocytic anemia with no evidence of hemolysis. Which metabolic mechanism is most likely contributing to his condition?

explanation.summary: Chronic NSAID use inhibits COX-1, reducing protective gastric prostaglandins and predisposing to ulcers and bleeding.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prolonged intake of an NSAID inhibits COX-1 and decreases production of prostaglandins such as PGE2 that are required for protective gastric mucus and reduced acid secretion. Loss of this mucosal protection leads to gastric bleeding and ulcers, a hallmark and major side effect of NSAID overuse, consistent with epigastric pain and a mild anemia from chronic blood loss.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Leukotrienes are not associated with adverse GI effects; although excess arachidonic acid could be shunted to LOX, that would not explain the gastric injury.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PLA2 activity and arachidonic acid generation would not be enhanced by COX inhibition, and increased substrate does not account for the mucosal damage.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inadequate erythrocyte NADPH causes hemolytic anemia (as in G6PD deficiency), but this patient has no evidence of hemolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: COX inhibition would reduce, not increase, thromboxane A2; excess TXA2 does not cause gastric ulceration.

questions.topicName: BOOM: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction (3 of 3)

questions.stem: A patient with a severe inflammatory condition is given a drug that broadly reduces production of prostaglandins, thromboxanes, and leukotrienes simultaneously by acting upstream in the arachidonic acid pathway. Which agent was most likely given?

explanation.summary: Glucocorticoids inhibit PLA2, blocking arachidonic acid release and dampening both the COX and LOX branches.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glucocorticoids such as cortisol and prednisone dampen inflammation by inhibiting the production of arachidonic acid via PLA2. Because arachidonic acid is the common substrate for both COX and LOX enzymes, blocking its release broadly suppresses prostaglandins, thromboxanes, and leukotrienes at once.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aspirin inhibits only the COX enzymes downstream of arachidonic acid; LOX-derived leukotrienes are not inhibited by COX blockade.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ibuprofen also inhibits only COX, so it does not reduce leukotriene formation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A leukotriene-receptor antagonist blocks only the LOX/leukotriene branch and does not reduce prostaglandins or thromboxanes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase to lower cholesterol synthesis and have no direct effect on the arachidonic acid pathway.

questions.topicName: BOOM: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction (3 of 3)

questions.stem: Several minutes after a bee sting, a patient who was given an antihistamine for immediate itching and hives develops worsening bronchoconstriction and inflammation that the antihistamine fails to control. This delayed but sustained reaction is mediated by molecules known as the slow-reacting substance of anaphylaxis. From which pathway are these mediators derived?

explanation.summary: Leukotrienes, the slow-reacting substance of anaphylaxis, are LOX-pathway products that cause delayed bronchoconstriction and inflammation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The lipoxygenase (LOX) pathway converts arachidonic acid into leukotrienes, which comprise the slow-reacting substance of anaphylaxis (SRS-A). Released from mast cells later than histamine and with a longer duration, leukotrienes cause pronounced bronchoconstriction and inflammation; because antihistamines do not block them, a steroid (which inhibits PLA2) is often warranted.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The COX pathway produces prostaglandins and thromboxanes, not the leukotrienes that make up SRS-A.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The PLC pathway produces second messengers that raise intracellular calcium and does not generate leukotrienes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The pentose phosphate pathway generates NADPH and ribose-5-phosphate and has no role in eicosanoid synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The HMG-CoA reductase pathway is involved in cholesterol synthesis, not leukotriene production.

questions.topicName: BOOM: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction (3 of 3)

questions.stem: An obstetrician selects an eicosanoid agent to induce labor and to help manage postpartum hemorrhage by stimulating uterine contractions. Which mediator has these actions?

explanation.summary: PGE2 stimulates uterine contractions, aiding labor induction and management of postpartum hemorrhage.
explanation.whyCorrect: Among the eicosanoids, PGE2 promotes labor induction and helps manage postpartum hemorrhage by stimulating uterine contractions. This reproductive role is one of the many physiological effects of prostaglandins produced through the COX pathway.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Thromboxane A2 causes platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction, not uterine contraction for labor induction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prostacyclin (PGI2) inhibits platelet aggregation and dilates blood vessels; it is not used to stimulate uterine contractions.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Leukotriene B4 is a LOX product that drives leukocyte chemotaxis and inflammation, not uterine contractions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Arachidonic acid is the upstream fatty acid precursor and has no direct receptor-mediated uterotonic action itself.

questions.topicName: BOOM: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction (3 of 3)

questions.stem: A cardiologist explains that low-dose aspirin permanently disables thromboxane production in platelets, yet vascular endothelial cells continue to make vasodilatory prostaglandins over time. Which feature of endothelial cells best explains their ability to overcome aspirin's inhibition and keep producing PGI2 and PGE2?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Endothelial cells contain organelles and can translate new COX-2 enzyme

choices.label: B
choices.text: Endothelial cells use LOX instead of COX to make prostaglandins

choices.label: C
choices.text: Endothelial cells lack COX enzymes entirely

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aspirin binds endothelial COX reversibly but platelet COX irreversibly

choices.label: E
choices.text: Endothelial cells convert thromboxane A2 into prostacyclin

explanation.summary: Because endothelial cells have organelles, they can synthesize new COX-2 and overcome aspirin's irreversible inhibition, unlike anucleate platelets.
explanation.whyCorrect: Unlike platelets, organelle-containing endothelial cells can translate new COX-2 enzyme, which overcomes the aspirin-mediated inhibition. This restored COX-2 produces PGE2 and PGI2, both of which promote vasodilation. Low-dose aspirin is chosen so that platelet COX-1 is permanently disabled while endothelial COX-2 can regenerate, forming the basis of low-dose aspirin therapy to lower stroke and heart-attack risk.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Prostaglandins including PGI2 are made by COX, not LOX; LOX makes leukotrienes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Endothelial cells do express COX (notably COX-2); they do not lack the enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspirin inhibits COX irreversibly in both cell types; the difference is that endothelium can make new enzyme while platelets cannot.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no enzymatic conversion of thromboxane A2 into prostacyclin; PGI2 is synthesized directly from the PGH2 precursor.

questions.topicName: BOOM: Lipids and Lipid Metabolism Introduction (3 of 3)

questions.stem: A patient with an inflammatory disorder that has not responded to other agents is prescribed indomethacin, a very potent NSAID. Compared with aspirin, how does indomethacin inhibit cyclooxygenase, and why does it tend to cause more gastric side effects?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Irreversible acetylation of COX; more gastric effects from higher COX-2 affinity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reversible competitive inhibition of COX; more gastric effects from higher COX-1 affinity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reversible inhibition of PLA2; more gastric effects from reduced leukotrienes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Irreversible inhibition of LOX; more gastric effects from increased prostaglandins

choices.label: E
choices.text: Selective irreversible inhibition of COX-2 only; more gastric effects from blocked PGI2

explanation.summary: Indomethacin is a reversible competitive COX inhibitor with higher affinity for COX-1, accounting for its greater gastric toxicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Unlike aspirin, indomethacin is not a suicide inhibitor; it is a very potent reversible, competitive COX inhibitor. Although it inhibits both isoforms, indomethacin has a higher affinity for COX-1, and greater COX-1 inhibition reduces the protective gastric prostaglandins, which accounts for its increased adverse gastric effects.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Irreversible acetylation describes aspirin, not indomethacin; and gastric toxicity relates to COX-1, not COX-2, inhibition.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Indomethacin inhibits COX, not PLA2, and its gastric effects stem from loss of protective prostaglandins, not reduced leukotrienes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Indomethacin inhibits COX, not LOX, and it decreases rather than increases prostaglandin production.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Indomethacin is not a COX-2 selective agent; loss of protective gastric prostaglandins comes from COX-1, not blocked PGI2 alone.
